content.assessment: Reforming admissions mechanisms while preserving track structure targets the specific finding that SES predicts track access independent of test scores more strongly in Hungary than elsewhere (F-live-education_finance-03), and does so without altering the fiscal envelope of the track system itself. This is a relatively low fiscal-magnitude intervention (procedural/administrative redesign rather than structural rebuilding) with plausible incidence-shifting effects, supported by the historical precedent that administrative desegregation rules produced measurable (if temporary) reductions in segregation.
opportunities: Complementary institution-by-institution accountability review could improve targeting of any additional resources without major new fiscal commitment
strengths: Directly targets the documented SES-independent sorting advantage without requiring new track infrastructure spending
Historical precedent (2000s administrative desegregation) demonstrates admissions-level levers can shift outcomes at plausibly lower cost than structural reform
threats: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside central authority, meaning any national admissions reform incurs a coverage gap that undermines uniform incidence correction
Reform fatigue risk observed previously suggests low durability of gains relative to implementation effort
weaknesses: Effect was temporary and 'stagnated mid-decade,' raising doubts about durable value for money from admissions-only reform
Divided union positions could slow implementation, extending the period during which current inequitable incidence persists
